wines.name: Tignanello
wines.oak_regime: 12 to 14 months in French and Hungarian oak barriques, around one third new

taste.summary: Sangiovese-led Super Tuscan with a Bordeaux skeleton: violet-and-cherry aromatics, firm graphite tannins and a long savoury finish that rewards bottle age.

pairings.why: Firm tannins cut through the marbling of a thick T-bone while the wine's cedar mirrors the charred crust.
Gamey ragu finds a peer in Tignanello's leather and dried-herb register.
Crystalline sheep's-milk salt sharpens the wine's red-cherry lift.
The Florentine peppered beef stew echoes the wine's spice register and matches its weight.

history.summary: Piero Antinori and Giacomo Tachis designed Tignanello as a deliberate break from Chianti Classico DOCG rules, blending Sangiovese with Cabernet and ageing in barrique. Declassified to vino da tavola at launch, the wine helped birth the Super Tuscan category and the eventual Toscana IGT.

milestones.event: First vintage released as vino da tavola, no Chianti Classico DOCG label
Cabernet Sauvignon added to the blend
Toscana IGT classification created, in part to accommodate wines like Tignanello

wines.description: Sangiovese-led Super Tuscan from Marchesi Antinori, first released in 1971. Barrique ageing on a Tuscan backbone, Toscana IGT. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Solaia
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques, predominantly new

taste.summary: Cabernet-led sibling of Tignanello, denser and more international in profile, with cassis and graphite intensity over a long Tuscan acid spine.

pairings.why: The wine's volume of fruit and oak demand a high-impact protein.

pairings.dish: Hard mountain cheeses with truffle honey
pairings.why: The wine's sweet cedar resonates with truffle aromatics.
Cabernet's iron-and-graphite mineral signature finds a partner in dry-aged beef umami.

history.summary: Launched seven years after Tignanello as the Cabernet-dominant counterpart from the same hillside at the Tenuta Tignanello estate. Wine Spectator named Solaia 1997 its Wine of the Year for 2000, the first Italian wine to win the title.

milestones.event: Wine Spectator names Solaia 1997 its Wine of the Year

wines.description: Cabernet-dominant Super Tuscan IGT from Marchesi Antinori's Tenuta Tignanello, first released 1978. Wine Spectator Wine of the Year 2000 (98 points). Decant ahead of pouring.

wines.name: Marchese Antinori Chianti Classico Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in French and Hungarian oak barriques

taste.summary: Classic Chianti Classico Riserva from the Tignanello estate's vineyards, sour-cherry-and-tobacco profile with the structure of the Marchese cuvee.

pairings.why: Riserva spice and game-leather aromatics frame the boar ragu.
Florentine chicken-liver crostini find a perfect host in Sangiovese acidity.
The Riserva tannins handle the marrow of a T-bone without overwhelming it.

history.summary: Antinori's flagship Chianti Classico Riserva, drawn from the same hillside as Tignanello but bottled inside the DOCG framework. Now classified at the Gran Selezione tier in select vintages.

milestones.event: First vintage of the modern Marchese cuvee

wines.description: Antinori's flagship Chianti Classico Riserva from the Tenuta Tignanello hillside, Sangiovese-dominant with a touch of Cabernet Sauvignon. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Villa Antinori Chianti Classico Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in barriques and tonneaux

taste.summary: Sangiovese-led Chianti Classico Riserva in a juicier, more accessible Antinori register, with cherry and tobacco at the core.

pairings.why: Hand-rolled pici pasta and meat ragu sit comfortably with this Riserva's mid-weight tannin.
The salty Tuscan flatbread frames the wine's red fruit.
Slow-stewed boar matches the Riserva's leather notes.

history.summary: One of the longest-running Antinori labels, in continuous production since 1928 and reformulated for Chianti Classico Riserva. The current blend draws fruit from multiple Antinori estates across the DOCG.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Villa Antinori label
Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and Petit Verdot added to the blend

wines.description: Antinori's longest-running Chianti Classico Riserva label, in continuous production since 1928 and Sangiovese-led across multiple estates in the DOCG.

wines.name: Castello di Ama Vigneto Bellavista
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in French oak tonneaux, partly new

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Gran Selezione from Castello di Ama's Bellavista parcel at Gaiole, with rose-petal aromatics and iron-mineral intensity.

pairings.why: Game bird and balsamic notes find a Bellavista counterpart.
The wine's iron echoes the cheese's minerality.
Bellavista's structure matches a charcoal T-bone.

history.summary: Single-vineyard Chianti Classico originally introduced as a Vigna designation in 1978; classified Gran Selezione from the 2010 vintage when the new DOCG tier was introduced.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Vigneto Bellavista single-vineyard
Reclassified to Chianti Classico Gran Selezione

wines.description: Castello di Ama's high-altitude Bellavista parcel at Gaiole yields this Chianti Classico Gran Selezione single-vineyard bottling. Decant ahead of pouring.

wines.name: Castello di Ama Vigneto La Casuccia
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in French oak tonneaux, partly new

taste.summary: The Merlot-leaning sibling to Bellavista, deeper-fruited with mocha and dark cherry through a long Sangiovese acid spine.

pairings.why: Cherry-on-cherry doubling intensifies the cuvee's fruit.
Slow-cooked beef and red wine reduction match the wine's plush mid-palate.
Florence's tripe-roll street food meets a Gran Selezione for an unexpected counterpoint.

history.summary: Reintroduced after a long pause as a single-vineyard Gran Selezione; the La Casuccia parcel adds a small dose of Merlot to the Sangiovese base for a deeper-fruited profile.

milestones.event: First vintage of the La Casuccia label

wines.description: From Castello di Ama's La Casuccia parcel, a Chianti Classico Gran Selezione where a small percentage of Merlot deepens the Sangiovese base.

wines.name: Castello di Ama Haiku
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in French oak barriques and tonneaux

taste.summary: Castello di Ama's Bordeaux-blend Toscana IGT with Sangiovese leading and Cabernet Franc adding aromatic lift.

pairings.why: Cabernet Franc's herbal lift meets the game and cocoa.
Iron-graphite minerality matches the earthy truffle.
Florentine tripe stew works against the wine's herbal Cabernet Franc lift.

history.summary: Introduced 2007 as Castello di Ama's Bordeaux-blend Toscana IGT, originally with significant international varietals and now anchored on Sangiovese with Cabernet Franc and Merlot.

wines.description: Sangiovese-led Toscana IGT from Castello di Ama, introduced in 2007 with Cabernet Franc and Merlot in support. A reference bottle for the appellation.

wines.name: Castello di Ama L'Apparita
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in French oak barriques, predominantly new

taste.summary: Castello di Ama's cult 100 percent Merlot Toscana IGT from a tiny Gaiole parcel, plush and graphite-mineral.

pairings.why: Merlot's plush texture matches tenderloin while graphite finds truffle.
Sweet umami of the cheese meets the wine's iron and dark fruit.
Game and cherry double the wine's dark-fruit register.

history.summary: A pure Merlot from a tiny high-altitude parcel at Gaiole, first vintage 1985. One of the first single-vineyard Merlots produced in Chianti Classico's heart.

wines.description: Castello di Ama's cult single-vineyard 100 percent Merlot Toscana IGT, first released 1985 from a small high-altitude Gaiole parcel. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Felsina Rancia Chianti Classico Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 20 months in French oak barriques and tonneaux

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Sangiovese Riserva from the limestone-rich Rancia parcel at Castelnuovo Berardenga; bone-dry, iron-mineral, age-worthy.

pairings.why: Rancia's iron mineral finds the game in the ragu.
Sangiovese acidity refreshes the slow stew.
Fresh sheep's-milk cheese sharpens the wine's red-fruit lift.

history.summary: Single-vineyard Riserva from the limestone-rich Rancia parcel at Castelnuovo Berardenga, first vintage 1983. Considered one of Chianti Classico's most consistently age-worthy bottles.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Rancia Vigna designation

wines.description: Single-vineyard 100 percent Sangiovese Chianti Classico Riserva from the limestone-rich Rancia parcel at Castelnuovo Berardenga, first released 1983.

wines.name: Felsina Fontalloro
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 20 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT from Felsina, denser and oakier than Rancia, blended across Chianti Classico and Colli Senesi vineyards.

pairings.why: Fontalloro's structure stands up to a charcoal-grilled T-bone.
Cold beef and iron-graphite meet on equal terms.
Earthy mushrooms find Fontalloro's leather and iron register.

history.summary: Felsina's pure Sangiovese IGT, launched alongside Rancia in 1983 from a multi-vineyard blend bridging Chianti Classico and Colli Senesi.

wines.description: Felsina at Castelnuovo Berardenga, this pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT was first released in 1983 from cross-appellation vineyards. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Felsina Berardenga Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 10 to 12 months in large Slavonian oak casks

taste.summary: The Felsina entry-level annata, pure Sangiovese in a translucent old-school register: sour cherry, violets, fennel.

pairings.why: Annata weight and red-fruit acidity sing with hand-rolled pici.
Chicken-liver paste and Sangiovese is a Florentine sacrament.
Bright acidity refreshes the tomato.

history.summary: Felsina's annata Chianti Classico has been bottled continuously since 1966 from the Berardenga estate's Sangiovese vineyards.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Berardenga Chianti Classico

wines.description: Felsina's annata Chianti Classico from the Berardenga estate, pure Sangiovese aged in large Slavonian casks since 1966. A reference bottle for the appellation.

wines.name: Fontodi Flaccianello della Pieve
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques, around one third new

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT from the Conca d'Oro amphitheatre at Panzano, polished and graphite-toned with extraordinary precision.

pairings.why: Flaccianello's elegance partners equally elegant beef.
The game-leather signature meets Flaccianello's spice register.
Crystalline cheese amplifies the wine's red-fruit acid.

history.summary: Giovanni Manetti's flagship pure Sangiovese, first released 1981 from the Conca d'Oro amphitheatre at Panzano. Helped legitimise 100 percent Sangiovese as a Super Tuscan idiom.

milestones.event: First vintage of Flaccianello della Pieve
Estate converted to certified organic farming

wines.description: Fontodi's flagship pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT from the Conca d'Oro at Panzano in Chianti, first vintage 1981 under Giovanni Manetti. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Fontodi Vigna del Sorbo
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Gran Selezione from Fontodi's Sorbo parcel, formerly a Cabernet blend and now pure Sangiovese.

pairings.why: Slow-braised peppered beef matches the Riserva's mid-weight spice.
Iron-graphite spine handles a thick T-bone.
Sangiovese acidity refreshes the rich red-wine sauce.

history.summary: Single-vineyard Vigna designation from Fontodi's Sorbo parcel, originally a Sangiovese-Cabernet Sauvignon Riserva. Reformulated to 100 percent Sangiovese from the 2015 vintage with the move to Gran Selezione.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Vigna del Sorbo Riserva
Reformulated as 100 percent Sangiovese Gran Selezione

wines.description: Fontodi's Sorbo parcel, reformulated as pure Sangiovese from 2015, bottled as Chianti Classico Gran Selezione. A reference bottle for the appellation.

wines.name: Fontodi Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Fontodi's annata, pure Sangiovese from organic vineyards at Panzano, with the house signature of violet and graphite.

pairings.why: Bright Sangiovese acidity meets ripe tomato perfectly.
Annata weight and tannin match hand-rolled pici with meat.
Cheese acidity sharpens cherry-and-fennel notes.

history.summary: Manetti family Chianti Classico, in continuous production since 1968 and certified organic since 1990 under ICEA.

milestones.event: Manetti family acquires Fontodi and begins bottling

wines.description: Fontodi's annata Chianti Classico, pure Sangiovese from organic vineyards at Panzano in Chianti, in production since 1968. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Isole e Olena Cepparello
wines.oak_regime: 20 to 22 months in French oak barriques, partly new

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT created by Paolo De Marchi to bottle the best vineyard at Olena outside DOCG strictures; rose-and-graphite precision.

pairings.why: Game-bird and chestnut sweetness meet Cepparello's red-fruit lift.
Iron-mineral spine for a charcoal-grilled T-bone.
Sweet umami amplifies the wine's mid-palate weight.

history.summary: Paolo De Marchi's flagship, born from a refusal to dilute his best Sangiovese with white grapes as Chianti Classico law required at the time. EPI Group acquired the estate in 2022; Cepparello continues under the new ownership.

wines.description: Paolo De Marchi's flagship pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT from the Olena estate at Barberino Tavarnelle, first vintage 1980. Cellar-door allocations sell out yearly.

wines.name: Isole e Olena Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Isole e Olena's annata Chianti Classico, a translucent Sangiovese-led blend with the house cherry-and-violet aromatics.

pairings.why: Acid-led Sangiovese refreshes ragu-coated pici.
Sour-cherry acidity matches tomato sweetness.
Florentine chicken-liver crostini classic with annata Sangiovese.

history.summary: The Olena estate's annata Chianti Classico has been bottled since the De Marchi family acquired the property in 1956.

milestones.event: De Marchi family acquires the Isole e Olena estate

wines.description: Annata Chianti Classico from Isole e Olena at Barberino Tavarnelle, Sangiovese-led with Canaiolo Nero and a sliver of Syrah. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Isole e Olena Collezione De Marchi Syrah
wines.oak_regime: 16 months in French oak barriques, partly new

taste.summary: Northern-Rhone-styled Syrah from the Olena estate, peppery and savoury with a graphite Tuscan spine.

pairings.why: Syrah's herbal lift mirrors the rosemary marinade.
Game-leather signature meets Syrah's smoke and pepper.
Fennel salame finds the wine's wild-herb register.

history.summary: Paolo De Marchi's pioneering Tuscan Syrah, planted from Rhone cuttings and bottled separately under the Collezione De Marchi label since 1991. Continues under EPI Group ownership.

milestones.event: First vintage of the De Marchi Syrah

wines.description: Pure Syrah Toscana IGT from the Isole e Olena estate, bottled as a separate Collezione since 1991 under the De Marchi label. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: San Felice Il Grigio Gran Selezione
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques and tonneaux

taste.summary: Sangiovese-led Gran Selezione from Castelnuovo Berardenga with a small slice of the rare indigenous Pugnitello grape.

pairings.why: Gran Selezione tannins frame the T-bone marbling.
Iron-graphite mineral resonates with boar ragu.
Sweet umami meets red-fruit lift.

history.summary: Originally a Vigorello-stable Sangiovese Riserva, now bottled at the Gran Selezione tier from the Poggio Rosso vineyards at Castelnuovo Berardenga. Includes a small percentage of Pugnitello, the indigenous grape recovered by San Felice from near-extinction.

milestones.event: First vintage of Il Grigio Riserva

wines.description: San Felice's flagship Chianti Classico Gran Selezione from Castelnuovo Berardenga, Sangiovese-led with the indigenous Pugnitello grape in support.

wines.name: San Felice Vigorello
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: One of the original Super Tuscans, Bordeaux-blended with a slice of the indigenous Pugnitello variety.

pairings.why: Cabernet weight finds dry-aged beef umami.

pairings.dish: Aged Pecorino Toscano with truffle honey
pairings.why: Sweet truffle honey amplifies the wine's cedar.

pairings.dish: Beef short rib in red wine
pairings.why: Bordeaux-blend structure handles braised short rib.

history.summary: Vigorello was first released in 1968, often cited as the first Super Tuscan. Originally a Sangiovese-Cabernet blend, now a Bordeaux-grape-plus-Pugnitello composition. Toscana IGT.

milestones.event: First vintage of Vigorello, often called the first Super Tuscan

wines.description: San Felice's pioneering Super Tuscan, first released 1968 and now a Bordeaux-blend plus Pugnitello in Toscana IGT. Best from glassware with a slight inward curl.

wines.name: San Felice Pugnitello
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: The flagship 100 percent Pugnitello bottling, deep and peppery from a grape San Felice rescued from extinction in the 1980s.

pairings.why: Pugnitello's natural pepper amplifies the crust.
Game and dark fruit lock together.
Truffle meets the wine's iron and leather.

history.summary: San Felice researchers identified Pugnitello as a distinct ancient varietal in 1981; the estate cloned vines and produced the first 100 percent Pugnitello bottling in 2003.

milestones.event: San Felice identifies Pugnitello as a distinct grape
First vintage of the 100 percent Pugnitello bottling

wines.description: Single-varietal Pugnitello Toscana IGT, the showcase of San Felice's recovery of this indigenous grape variety from near-extinction. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Castello di Volpaia Coltassala
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques and tonneaux

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Gran Selezione from the 600-metre Coltassala parcel at Radda, with a touch of Mammolo for aromatic perfume.

pairings.why: Mountain pecorino sharpens Coltassala's red-fruit acid.
Hand-rolled pici match the wine's mid-weight tannin.
Game bird and wild-fennel notes complement.

history.summary: Coltassala was bottled separately as a Vigna designation since 1980 from a single parcel at 600 metres. Promoted to Gran Selezione in 2014.

wines.description: High-altitude Coltassala parcel at Radda, with Mammolo in support, bottled as a Chianti Classico Gran Selezione suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Castello di Volpaia Il Puro Gran Selezione
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: 100 percent Sangiovese Gran Selezione from a single Radda vineyard, austere and graphite-toned.

pairings.why: Iron mineral spine handles a charcoal T-bone.
Game meat finds the wine's wild-herb register.
Cheese acidity sharpens cherry-and-fennel notes.

history.summary: Volpaia's first pure Sangiovese Vigna selection, launched 2007 from a single Radda parcel at 600 metres and classified Gran Selezione in 2014.

wines.description: Pure Sangiovese Chianti Classico Gran Selezione from Castello di Volpaia at Radda in Chianti, first vintage 2007. Best from glassware with a slight inward curl.

wines.name: Castello di Volpaia Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Annata Chianti Classico from the 600-metre Volpaia hamlet, translucent and herbal with the high-altitude Radda signature.

pairings.why: Cherry-and-tomato is the Chianti annata sweet spot.
Florentine chicken-liver crostini, Sangiovese acidity refreshes.
Tomato sweetness meets sour-cherry acid.

history.summary: Volpaia's annata is the village's daily wine, produced from the 600-metre Radda vineyards in continuous bottling since 1980.

milestones.event: First vintage of the modern Volpaia Chianti Classico

wines.description: Annata Chianti Classico from the 600-metre hilltop hamlet of Volpaia, ICEA-organic Sangiovese-led blend bottled since 1980. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Querciabella Camartina
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 22 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: Sangiovese-Cabernet Toscana IGT from biodynamic Greve in Chianti vineyards, polished and graphite-toned.

pairings.why: Bordeaux-blend structure meets lamb fat.
Camartina's spice register frames boar ragu.
Sweet umami amplifies the cedar register.

history.summary: Querciabella's flagship Super Tuscan, first released 1981. Estate fully Demeter-certified biodynamic since 2000; vegan vinification since 2011.

wines.description: Querciabella's biodynamic Sangiovese-Cabernet Super Tuscan Toscana IGT from Greve in Chianti, first vintage 1981. Best from glassware with a slight inward curl.

wines.name: Querciabella Batar
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in French oak barriques, partial malolactic

taste.summary: Tuscany's most age-worthy oak-aged white, a Chardonnay-Pinot Bianco blend modelled on Burgundy.

pairings.why: Roast chicken classic with oak-aged white.
Hazelnut and wet stone marry the porcini.

pairings.dish: Lardo di Colonnata on toasted bread
pairings.why: Cured pork fat finds the wine's salty mineral.

history.summary: Sebastiano Castiglioni created Batar in 1988 as Tuscany's answer to white Burgundy, a Chardonnay-Pinot Bianco blend in barrique. Toscana IGT.

wines.description: Chardonnay-Pinot Bianco Toscana IGT from biodynamic Querciabella in Greve in Chianti, first vintage 1988 and Tuscany's age-worthy white benchmark.

wines.name: Querciabella Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese annata from biodynamic Greve in Chianti vineyards, mid-weight and herbal.

pairings.why: Annata weight handles cheese-and-pepper pasta.
Florentine tripe stew is a Sangiovese match.
Chicken-liver paste classic.

history.summary: Querciabella's annata Chianti Classico from biodynamic Greve in Chianti vineyards, vegan vinified since 2011.

milestones.event: Estate adopts vegan vinification (no animal fining agents)

wines.description: Annata Chianti Classico from biodynamic Demeter-certified Querciabella in Greve in Chianti, pure Sangiovese with vegan vinification. Built for cellaring.

wines.name: Castell'in Villa Chianti Classico Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 24 to 30 months in large Slavonian oak casks

taste.summary: Old-school Sangiovese Riserva from Castelnuovo Berardenga, long-aged in large Slavonian botti.

pairings.why: Tobacco and game-bird flavours echo.
Old-vine Sangiovese handles the T-bone.
Crystalline cheese matches red-fruit acid.

history.summary: Coralia Pignatelli has produced traditionally styled Chianti Classico Riserva at Castell'in Villa since 1971. Bottlings routinely sleep a decade before release.

wines.description: Traditionally styled Chianti Classico Riserva from Castell'in Villa at Castelnuovo Berardenga, pure Sangiovese long-aged in Slavonian casks since 1971.

wines.name: Castell'in Villa Poggio delle Rose Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 30 to 36 months in large Slavonian oak casks

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Gran Selezione from a tiny Castell'in Villa parcel, library-release timing and long Slavonian-cask ageing.

pairings.why: Library bottle and white truffle is a contemplative match.
Old-vine fruit handles crystalline cheese salt.
Game-leather signature meets sweet chestnut.

history.summary: Single-vineyard Sangiovese from the high-elevation Poggio delle Rose parcel, first vintage 1985 and reclassified Gran Selezione in 2014.

milestones.event: First vintage of Poggio delle Rose

wines.description: Castell'in Villa's Poggio delle Rose parcel, bottled as a Chianti Classico Gran Selezione and library-released after long Slavonian-cask ageing.

wines.name: Castell'in Villa Santacroce
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques and Slavonian casks

taste.summary: Sangiovese-Cabernet blend from Castell'in Villa, the estate's modern Bordeaux-leaning IGT.

pairings.why: Cabernet weight handles lamb fat.
Sweet umami amplifies the wine's mid-palate.
Blend structure handles a T-bone.

history.summary: Sangiovese-Cabernet IGT from Castell'in Villa, first vintage around 1990 as the estate's Super Tuscan bottling.

wines.description: Sangiovese-Cabernet Toscana IGT from Castell'in Villa, a Bordeaux-leaning Super Tuscan with traditional Slavonian-cask ageing. Decant ahead of pouring.

wines.name: Riecine La Gioia
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Riecine's flagship pure Sangiovese IGT from Gaiole, vegan-vinified and ICEA-organic, with rose-and-iron precision.

pairings.why: Herbal lift in the wine echoes the dish.
Game classic for Riecine's mid-weight tannin.
Crystalline cheese sharpens the wine's red-fruit acid.

history.summary: Englishman John Dunkley created La Gioia in 1982 as Riecine's pure Sangiovese Super Tuscan from Gaiole. Estate now owned by Lana Frank with vegan vinification and ICEA-organic farming.

wines.description: Riecine's flagship pure Sangiovese Toscana IGT from Gaiole in Chianti, vegan-vinified, ICEA-organic since 2014, first vintage 1982. Suited to long ageing.

wines.name: Riecine Chianti Classico Riserva
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese Riserva from Riecine's Gaiole vineyards, the structure of La Gioia with shorter ageing.

pairings.why: Riserva tannins match meat-pasta weight.
T-bone classic with mid-weight Sangiovese Riserva.
Game stew finds the wine's leather notes.

history.summary: Riecine reintroduced a Chianti Classico Riserva in 1995 as the bridge between annata and La Gioia.

wines.description: Pure Sangiovese Chianti Classico Riserva from Riecine in Gaiole, ICEA-organic with vegan vinification. Drinks best from glassware that opens out at the lip.

wines.name: Riecine Chianti Classico
wines.oak_regime: 10 to 12 months in French oak tonneaux

taste.summary: Pure Sangiovese annata from Riecine's tiny 13-hectare estate at Gaiole, vegan-vinified.

pairings.why: Tomato sweetness meets sour-cherry acidity.
Florentine tripe stew, Sangiovese acidity sings.
Wood-fired pizza with weekday Chianti.

history.summary: John Dunkley founded Riecine in 1971 and bottled the first annata in 1976. Today the 13-hectare estate is owned by Lana Frank with vegan vinification and ICEA-organic farming.

wines.description: Pure Sangiovese annata Chianti Classico from Riecine in Gaiole in Chianti, vegan-vinified and ICEA-organic. Decant an hour ahead of pouring.

wines.name: Castello di Querceto Il Picchio
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Gran Selezione from the Picchio parcel at Lucolena, Sangiovese-led with a small Cabernet support.

pairings.why: Picchio's structure matches a T-bone.
Sangiovese and game-leather classic.
Cheese acidity refreshes the wine's cherry register.

history.summary: Castello di Querceto's Vigna designation from the Picchio parcel at Lucolena in Greve, first vintage 1988 and Gran Selezione from 2010.

wines.description: Greve in Chianti's Picchio parcel from Castello di Querceto, bottled as a Chianti Classico Gran Selezione. A reference bottle for the appellation.

wines.name: Castello di Querceto La Corte
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in French oak barriques

pairings.dish: Roast chicken with lemon and herbs
pairings.why: Crisp roast skin and herb butter mirror the wine's freshness and dried-herb lift.

history.summary: Brolio Bettino honours Bettino Ricasoli, who codified the Chianti formula at Brolio in 1872. The cuvee was first bottled in 2013 to revive the original recipe: Sangiovese plus a small Abrusco share, aged in big casks rather than barrique. Both James Suckling and Robert Parker's Wine Advocate scored the 2013 vintage 92 in 2016.

milestones.event: Bettino Ricasoli writes down the modern Chianti formula

wines.description: A 90 percent Sangiovese / 10 percent Abrusco Chianti Classico DOCG honouring Bettino Ricasoli's 1872 Chianti formula. Aged 18 months in large casks. Traditionalist, food-driven Brolio.

taste.summary: The canonical pure-Sangiovese IGT. Red cherry, rose and dried herbs over a tobacco-and-iron core; long, linear finish that rewards a decade or more in bottle.

pairings.why: Charred T-bone needs the firm Sangiovese tannin and the dried-herb lift cuts the marbling.
Pepper-and-pecorino pasta meets the wine's tobacco-and-iron savouriness.

pairings.dish: Aged pecorino toscano with chestnut honey
pairings.why: Pecorino salinity sharpens the rose-and-cherry aromatics; chestnut honey echoes the leather.

history.summary: Sergio Manetti released the first Le Pergole Torte in 1977 as the first pure Sangiovese vinified in the Chianti zone. The producer left the Chianti Classico DOCG consortium in 1981 and chose Toscana IGT (and Vino da Tavola historically) on principle, even as the wine became one of Italy's reference Sangioveses. Today Martino Manetti runs the estate.

pairings.dish: Roast leg of lamb with rosemary
pairings.why: Lamb fat finds the firm Sangiovese tannin; rosemary echoes the herbal lift.
Sheep's-milk salt sharpens the wine's sour-cherry brightness.

history.summary: Sergio Manetti's Montevertine bottling first appeared in 1971 as a Chianti Classico Riserva. After the 1981 consortium withdrawal it was reclassified, and from 1982 it has carried the simple Montevertine name on the Toscana IGT bottle. The field-blend recipe of Sangiovese with small Canaiolo and Colorino shares has been unchanged since.

milestones.event: First Montevertine vintage, bottled as Chianti Classico
Estate withdraws from Chianti Classico consortium
Wine is renamed simply Montevertine, reclassified as Vino da Tavola then Toscana IGT

wines.description: The Montevertine field-blend Toscana IGT: 90 percent Sangiovese with Canaiolo and Colorino, aged 24 months in Slavonian botti. The estate's namesake bottle.

wines.name: Pian del Ciampolo
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in Slavonian oak casks after fermentation in concrete

taste.summary: The everyday Montevertine. Same field blend, shorter cask, brighter cherry and softer tannin: built for a Tuesday table.

pairings.why: The wine's red-cherry brightness and medium tannin meet tomato acid and mozzarella creaminess.
Beef-and-tomato ragu calls for Sangiovese's red fruit and acid lift.
Sage and roast chicken with the wine's herbal core is a Radda weeknight.

history.summary: Pian del Ciampolo entered the Montevertine line-up around 1991 as a younger-drinking bottling drawn from across the estate's parcels. Same field-blend recipe as the namesake Montevertine; shorter wood means earlier accessibility and a lower price.

wines.description: Montevertine's weeknight bottling. Same Sangiovese-Canaiolo-Colorino recipe as the namesake wine, with shorter cask ageing for earlier drinking.

wines.name: Poggio di Sotto Brunello di Montalcino
wines.oak_regime: Around 36 to 48 months in large Slavonian oak casks (botti grandi)

taste.summary: Classical southeastern Montalcino Brunello: rose-and-dried-cherry aromatics, iron-and-leather core, finely chiselled tannins, very long finish.

pairings.why: Porcini umami amplifies the wine's leather and dried-herb register.
Game fat meets the wine's firm tannin; juniper mirrors the Mediterranean-herb core.

pairings.dish: Aged pecorino toscano with truffle honey
pairings.why: Pecorino salt sharpens the rose-and-cherry aromatics; truffle honey echoes the wine's earthy depth.

history.summary: Piero Palmucci founded Poggio di Sotto in 1989 and released the first Brunello in 1991, vinifying with Giulio Gambelli in the traditionalist Montalcino style: long Slavonian-cask ageing, restrained extraction, late picking. ColleMassari Group (Maria Iris Bertarelli and Claudio Tipa) acquired the estate in 2011, preserving the house style.

milestones.event: Piero Palmucci founds Poggio di Sotto at Castelnuovo dell'Abate
First Poggio di Sotto Brunello vintage

wines.description: Castelnuovo dell'Abate Brunello in the traditionalist long-Slavonian-cask style. Founded by Piero Palmucci in 1989; ColleMassari Group-owned since 2011.

wines.name: Poggio di Sotto Brunello di Montalcino Riserva
wines.oak_regime: Around 60 months in large Slavonian oak casks, released only in exceptional vintages

taste.summary: The Riserva amplifies the house signature: rose-petal and dried-cherry depth, iron-and-leather backbone, a very long balsamic finish. Released only in exceptional vintages.

pairings.why: Fat-rich beef demands the Riserva's firm tannin; marrow umami amplifies the wine's leather.
Traditional hare stew finds a peer in the wine's dried-fig and balsamic register.
Black-truffle aromatics weave into the wine's potpourri and rose-petal lift.

history.summary: Poggio di Sotto bottles a Riserva only in exceptional years, drawn from the longest-cask selection of the estate's best parcels. The wine is widely cited in modern Brunello critic top-fives. ColleMassari Group has continued the Palmucci-era house style since the 2011 acquisition.

milestones.event: First Poggio di Sotto Brunello Riserva vintage
Estate transitions to ColleMassari Group ownership

wines.description: The Poggio di Sotto Brunello Riserva, released only in exceptional vintages. Around 60 months in large Slavonian botti. A modern reference Brunello Riserva.

wines.name: Poggio di Sotto Rosso di Montalcino
wines.oak_regime: Around 24 months in large Slavonian oak casks

taste.summary: A Rosso di Montalcino in Brunello clothing: same southeastern fruit and Slavonian-cask discipline, shorter wood, brighter cherry and earlier accessibility.

pairings.why: Hand-rolled pici with boar ragu lands cleanly on the wine's leather-and-cherry profile.
Lamb fat and rosemary meet the wine's medium tannin and herbal lift.
Sheep's-milk salt sharpens the wine's sour-cherry brightness.

history.summary: Poggio di Sotto's Rosso di Montalcino is bottled to the same exacting standards as the Brunello, drawn from the same Castelnuovo dell'Abate fruit but aged on a shorter timeline. Often cited as one of the most cellar-worthy Rosso DOC wines in Montalcino.

milestones.event: First Poggio di Sotto Rosso di Montalcino vintage

wines.description: Poggio di Sotto's Rosso di Montalcino DOC: Brunello-grade fruit on a shorter Slavonian-cask timeline. A reference for the DOC tier.

wines.name: Castello Fonterutoli Chianti Classico Gran Selezione
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 500-litre French oak barrels, 50 percent new; four months in concrete tank before bottling

taste.summary: The Mazzei flagship: 100 percent Sangiovese from six clones plus eight massal selections, with a forward black-cherry and graphite profile and firm structural backbone.

pairings.why: The wine's firm tannin cuts the marbling; cedar and graphite mirror the charred crust.

pairings.dish: Wild boar stew with chestnut polenta
pairings.why: Game ragu and sweet chestnut meet the wine's dark-cherry and herbal core.
Sheep's-milk salinity sharpens the wine's lift; mostarda echoes the sweet-spice register.

history.summary: Filippo and Francesco Mazzei launched Castello Fonterutoli in 1995 as the new top of the estate's Sangiovese pyramid. The wine became one of the forerunners of the Chianti Classico Gran Selezione category, formally created in 2014.

milestones.event: Chianti Classico Gran Selezione tier launched; Castello Fonterutoli a forerunner

wines.description: Castello Fonterutoli Chianti Classico Gran Selezione DOCG: 100 percent Sangiovese from six clones plus eight massal selections, 18 months in 500-litre French oak. Mazzei flagship.

wines.name: Siepi
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in small French oak with 70 percent new (Sangiovese in tonneaux, Merlot in barriques); three months in concrete tank before bottling

taste.summary: A 50/50 Sangiovese-Merlot Super Tuscan from the historic Siepi vineyard at 850 feet. Black plum, sandalwood and tobacco over a firm cedar-and-cherry core.

pairings.dish: Braised beef cheeks with red wine
pairings.why: Long-cooked beef cheek finds a peer in the wine's volume and sweet cedar.
Game fat meets the Bordeaux side; juniper mirrors the Mediterranean lift.

pairings.dish: Hard aged cheese plate with mostarda
pairings.why: Mixed pecorino and Parmigiano salinity sharpens the wine's dark-fruit core.

history.summary: Siepi was first bottled in 1992 from the historic single vineyard at Fonterutoli that the Mazzei family has owned since 1435. Gambero Rosso later listed it as one of the 50 wines that changed Italian wine style.

milestones.event: Gambero Rosso lists Siepi as one of the 50 wines that changed Italian wine

wines.description: Mazzei's 50/50 Sangiovese-Merlot Super Tuscan Toscana IGT from the Siepi single vineyard at Fonterutoli. First vintage 1992.

wines.name: Concerto di Fonterutoli
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in small French oak: tonneaux for Sangiovese, barriques for Cabernet Sauvignon; three months in concrete tank

taste.summary: One of the original Super Tuscans, born 1981. Sangiovese-Cabernet blend with juicy blackberry, underbrush and graphite, capable of cellaring beyond 20 years.

pairings.why: Cabernet's cedar finds the char; Sangiovese acid cuts the marbling.
Sangiovese's earthy lift on game ragu, with Cabernet weight on the back palate.
Crystalline Parmigiano salt sharpens the wine; balsamic mirrors the sweet-spice register.

history.summary: Concerto was first bottled in 1981 as part of Mazzei's experimental phase with new training systems and grape blends, and was one of the early Super Tuscans. The wine has retained an 80/20 Sangiovese-Cabernet recipe.

milestones.event: First Concerto di Fonterutoli vintage, one of the original Super Tuscans

wines.description: One of the original Super Tuscans (1981). Mazzei's 80/20 Sangiovese-Cabernet Toscana IGT, with the 1981 launch placing it alongside Tignanello and Sassicaia in the founding wave.

wines.name: Poggio Badiola
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in 225-litre American oak barrels

taste.summary: An everyday Mazzei: Sangiovese-led with Merlot softness, brief American-oak round, made for a Tuesday-night table.

pairings.why: Tomato acid meets the wine's red-cherry brightness and high-acid lift.
Soft Merlot tannin and Sangiovese acid both work with tomato-mozzarella.
Casalinga sausage smoke meets the wine's vanilla-oak round and red-fruit core.

history.summary: Poggio Badiola entered the Mazzei range in the late 1990s as the everyday Toscana IGT below the estate's Chianti Classico and Super Tuscan tiers.

wines.description: Mazzei's everyday Sangiovese-Merlot Toscana IGT. Ten months in 225-litre American oak. Built for weeknight pizza and pasta.

wines.name: Galatrona
wines.classification: Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC (Vigna Galatrona)
wines.oak_regime: 18 to 20 months in French oak barriques, around one third new, with batonnage of fine lees in the first six months

taste.summary: Italy's reference 100 percent Merlot away from Bolgheri. Black cherry, blackberry and violet over a graphite-iron mineral spine, with polished oak and firm tannin in support.

pairings.dish: Roasted duck breast with cherry reduction
pairings.why: Duck fat meets the wine's polished oak; cherry reduction echoes the dark-fruit core.
Long-cooked beef collagen finds a peer in the wine's volume and firm tannin.

pairings.dish: Aged Tuscan pecorino with truffle honey
pairings.why: Truffle aromatics weave into the wine's iron mineral note; pecorino salt sharpens the fruit.

history.summary: Galatrona was first bottled in 1994 from a 10-hectare blue-clay parcel planted to low-vigour Bordeaux clones in the late 1980s through mid-1990s. Vinified with native yeasts and spontaneous malolactic fermentation. Petrolo classifies it as the 'Grand-Cru' of Merlot from the Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC.

milestones.event: Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC formally recognised

wines.description: Italy's reference 100 percent Merlot outside Bolgheri. Petrolo's Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC bottling from the 10-hectare Galatrona parcel, first released 1994.

wines.name: Boggina C
wines.classification: Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC (Vigna Boggina)
wines.oak_regime: 16 to 18 months in French oak, 22hL barrels and 7hL tonneaux, after spontaneous malolactic fermentation in wood

taste.summary: Petrolo's pure Sangiovese cru from the Boggina parcel. Red-cherry and dried-herb aromatics on an iron-and-leather core; longer-format oak preserves transparency.

pairings.why: Charred T-bone needs the firm Sangiovese tannin; iron-and-leather meets the marbling.
Wild boar finds the wine's herbal-leather register.
Sheep's-milk salt sharpens the cherry; mostarda echoes the wine's spice.

history.summary: Boggina C is Petrolo's Sangiovese cru bottling from the Boggina parcel, first released in 2006. Spontaneous malolactic fermentation in wood and larger-format oak (22hL barrels and 7hL tonneaux) preserve transparency.

milestones.event: First Boggina C vintage from the single Boggina parcel

wines.description: Petrolo's pure Sangiovese cru from the Boggina parcel. Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC, longer-format oak, first released 2006.

wines.name: Torrione
wines.oak_regime: 15 to 18 months in concrete tanks, 40hL French oak barrels, tonneaux and barriques after spontaneous malolactic in wood

taste.summary: Petrolo's signature Sangiovese-Merlot-Cabernet blend, drawing from Boggina, Galatrona and Campo Lusso. The everyday face of the estate.

pairings.why: Sangiovese acid cuts the roast; cedar from the Cabernet share matches rosemary.
Porcini umami amplifies the wine's leather and earth; cherry brightens.
Cured pork fat meets the wine's medium tannin; tobacco mirrors the cured note.

history.summary: Torrione was launched in 1988 under Lucia Bazzocchi Sanjust's direction as part of Petrolo's shift toward premium winemaking. The wine has remained the estate's flagship blend, drawing from the historic Boggina (Sangiovese), Galatrona (Merlot) and Campo Lusso (Cabernet Sauvignon) parcels.

milestones.event: First Torrione vintage under Lucia Bazzocchi Sanjust

wines.description: Petrolo's signature Val d'Arno di Sopra DOC blend: 80/15/5 Sangiovese-Merlot-Cabernet Sauvignon. First released 1988. The everyday face of the estate.

wines.name: Cesani Vernaccia di San Gimignano
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel, no oak

taste.summary: Textbook Pancole Vernaccia: green-apple and lemon-zest brightness with the indigenous almond-bitter finish and wet-stone salinity.

pairings.why: Chicken-liver crostini and Vernaccia is a Tuscan local rite; bitterness of liver meets almond.

pairings.dish: Lardo di Colonnata on warm bread
pairings.why: Cured pork fat finds the wine's almond bitter and white-pepper lift.
Lemon acid mirrors the wine's citrus; salinity meets the fish.

history.summary: The Cesani Vernaccia has been bottled since the 1960s from the family's Pancole vineyards 6 km from San Gimignano. Vincenzo Cesani and the next-generation Cesani sisters (Letizia, Marialuisa, Sara) farm organically across 22 hectares.

milestones.event: Guido and Annunziata Cesani acquire the first land parcel at Pancole

wines.description: Cesani's annata Vernaccia di San Gimignano DOCG from the family's organic Pancole vineyards. Stainless steel, textbook expression of the indigenous Tuscan white.

wines.name: Cesani Clamys Vernaccia di San Gimignano
wines.oak_regime: Around six months on the lees in stainless steel and concrete, brief used-oak aging in some vintages

taste.summary: The intense Vernaccia from 40-plus-year-old Pancole vines. More yellow-fruit weight and saline length than the annata, with the indigenous almond signature.

pairings.why: Pepper-and-pecorino pasta meets the wine's saline and almond bitter.

pairings.dish: Roast chicken with sage and lemon
pairings.why: Roast chicken skin and sage on Vernaccia's ripe apple and high acid.
Pecorino salt sharpens the candied-citrus lift; chestnut honey echoes the honeycomb note.

history.summary: Clamys is Cesani's premium Vernaccia bottling, drawn from the oldest Pancole vines (planted from the early 1980s). Longer lees ageing and a brief used-oak step in selected vintages give the wine yellow-fruit weight and saline length.

wines.description: Cesani's premium Vernaccia di San Gimignano DOCG from old Pancole vines, with longer lees ageing for yellow-fruit weight and saline length.

wines.name: Cesani Luenzo
wines.oak_regime: Around 18 months in French oak tonneaux, partly new

taste.summary: The Cesani family's Toscana IGT red. Sangiovese-led with Merlot weight, organic Pancole fruit, tonneaux ageing for soft tannin and savoury depth.

pairings.why: Marbling and char meet the wine's medium tannin and red-cherry lift.
Game ragu and Sangiovese acid is a Tuscan classic; Merlot fills out the back palate.
Pecorino salt sharpens the cherry; chestnut honey echoes the sweet-spice register.

history.summary: Luenzo entered the Cesani range in the early 2000s as the family's Toscana IGT red counterpart to the Vernaccia. Drawn from the spiritually significant Luenzo parcel, the wine is the estate's main red ambassador.

wines.description: The Cesani family's Sangiovese-led Toscana IGT red. Tonneaux ageing for soft tannin and savoury depth; from organic Pancole fruit.

wines.name: Cesani Chianti Colli Senesi
wines.oak_regime: Around 10 months in stainless steel and large old wood

taste.summary: Cesani's everyday Chianti from the Colli Senesi sub-zone south-west of San Gimignano. Field-blend Sangiovese with bright red-cherry, tomato-leaf and rosemary lift.

pairings.why: Tomato-leaf in the wine echoes the sauce; Sangiovese acid keeps it bright.
Tomato and mozzarella with Sangiovese's red cherry and rosemary lift.
Cured pork meets the wine's medium tannin; rosemary mirrors the salame herbs.

history.summary: Cesani has bottled Chianti Colli Senesi since the early 1970s. The Colli Senesi sub-zone covers the Sangiovese hills south of San Gimignano and around Siena; Cesani farms organically and field-blends Sangiovese with small Canaiolo and Colorino shares.

milestones.event: First Cesani Chianti Colli Senesi bottling

wines.description: Cesani's everyday Chianti Colli Senesi DOCG from the field-blend Sangiovese hills south of San Gimignano. Organic, large-old-wood, weeknight-friendly.
